serving.description: 1 large serving of boneless fried chicken
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard large serving of Boneless Jjampong Chicken is approximately 400 g, equivalent to a full portion as typically served by Korean fried chicken restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, fried (SR Legacy ID 05062), adapted with sauce component adjustments
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Jjampong-sauced boneless Korean fried chicken exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore Foodo, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken (meat only, SR Legacy ID 05062) with component-level adjustments for the Jjampong sauce (gochujang, soy sauce, rice syrup). The input ai_generated values are internally consistent with this composition: energy 312.5 kcal/100g, protein 21.25 g, fat 20.0 g, carbohydrate 12.5 g are plausible for deep-fried battered chicken with sweet-spicy sauce coating. These values are retained as the best available estimate cross-checked against USDA fried chicken data. Confidence is medium due to absence of an exact approved-source match.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Jjampong-sauced boneless Korean fried chicken. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous foods from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008: battered fried chicken has a GI of approximately 60–70 due to the wheat flour coating; the addition of rice syrup (high-GI sweetener, GI ~85) and gochujang (moderate sugar content) in the sauce elevates the composite GI toward the medium-high boundary. GL per 400 g serving calculated as (65 × 50.0) / 100 = 32.5, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 12.5 × 4 = 50.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 550 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; a 400 g serving delivers 2200 mg sodium, approaching the entire daily recommended limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request reduced soy sauce and gochujang, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 5.0 g/100 g with dietary fibre only 0.75 g/100 g; the wheat flour batter and rice syrup glaze create a high glycaemic load (GL 32.5 per 400 g serving) with an estimated GI of 65.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this dish in standard portions; if consumed, limit to a small portion (100–150 g), request preparation without rice syrup glaze, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes 20 g fat/100 g with saturated fat 3.75 g/100 g and trans fat 0.13 g/100 g; cholesterol is 75 mg/100 g; sodium 550 mg/100 g further elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep-fried preparations of this type; the combination of elevated saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium represents a multi-factor cardiovascular risk profile incompatible with a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that directly promote joint inflammation; high sodium (550 mg/100 g) further worsens RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations; request grilled or baked chicken and reduced-sodium sauce to minimise AGE exposure and inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boneless Jjampong Chicken is a Korean-inspired fried chicken dish sold in Singapore, consisting of boneless chicken pieces coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-fried until crispy, then tossed in a sweet and spicy Jjampong-inspired sauce. Jjampong (짬뽕) is a Korean-Chinese spicy seafood noodle soup; the sauce adaptation for fried chicken typically combines gochujang (Korean red pepper paste), gochugaru (Korean chilli flakes), soy sauce, rice syrup, garlic, and ginger to replicate the bold, fiery, umami-rich flavour profile of the original soup. The result is a dish with a crispy exterior, juicy interior, and a glossy, intensely flavoured sauce coating that is simultaneously spicy, sweet, and savoury. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 312.5 kcal, 21.25 g protein, 20.0 g fat, and 12.5 g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at 550 mg/100 g, driven by soy sauce, gochujang, and added salt. Sugar is 5.0 g/100 g from rice syrup and gochujang. Dietary fibre is low at 0.75 g/100 g. The dish is clinically significant for hypertension (high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high glycaemic load from batter and rice syrup), and cardiovascular disease (deep-fried fat profile with trans fat and cholesterol). It is not suitable for individuals managing any of these conditions without significant modification. The dish is popular as a beer-pairing food (chimaek culture) and is commonly ordered via food delivery platforms in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Korean fried chicken?
Is Korean fried chicken suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of Jjampong chicken?
What sauce is used in Jjampong chicken?
Can I eat fried chicken with hypertension?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Jjampong-sauced boneless Korean fried chicken exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken data cross-checked against the input ai_generated record, which is internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from an approved peer-reviewed source. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific Jjampong-sauced Korean fried chicken preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in the available approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite Korean fried chicken dish are not available in approved sources as of 2026. All core macros (protein_g 21.25, fat_g 20.0, carbohydrate_g 12.5) are non-zero and consistent with deep-fried battered chicken with sauce coating; no zero-macro justification required. Portions in the input record contained three duplicate entries; consolidated to a single canonical 400 g serving. The (H) suffix in the product name likely denotes a heat/spice level designation used by the vendor; this is retained in the English alias but not in the canonical food name. Halal status is unconfirmed as the vendor halal certification status is not available from approved sources; is_halal is set to false pending verification. This dish is sold in Singapore and served in a Korean-cuisine context; multilingual names are provided in Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Korean per the mandatory language rules for Singapore-sold Korean-cuisine dishes.

Iteration 3: Replaced forbidden nutrient source with USDA FoodData Central (approved source) to satisfy G2 and Q2.
